# Verify that `sesam init --sesam-dir <subdir>` writes git-config driver
# entries that carry the `--sesam-dir=<subdir>` flag. Git invokes those
# drivers/aliases with cwd = worktree root, so without the flag they would
# fail to find the nested `.sesam/` tree.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

mkdir sub
exec sesam init --user admin --sesam-dir sub

# The driver subsection is suffixed with the (encoded) relative sesam dir, so
# multiple sesam repos in one git repo don't collide. Its command still carries
# --sesam-dir=sub so the binary resolves the nested .sesam/ when git invokes it
# from the worktree root.
exec git config --local diff.sesam-diff-sub.textconv
stdout '^sesam --sesam-dir=sub show$'
# The unsuffixed subsection must NOT exist for a nested repo.
! exec git config --local diff.sesam-diff.textconv

# The alias is a plain convenience shim, never repo-specific.
exec git config --local alias.sesam
stdout '^!sesam$'

exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# End-to-end: git runs its drivers/aliases with cwd = worktree root, passing the
# baked-in --sesam-dir=sub. Invoke sesam the same way (from the worktree root
# with that flag) and confirm it resolves the nested repo and lists the README
# init created under sub/. We call sesam directly rather than via `git sesam` so
# the check doesn't depend on the ambient shell's PATH resolving `sesam` (the
# git alias path is covered by git_sesam_alias_workflow.txt).
exec sesam --sesam-dir=sub ls
stdout 'README\.md'
stdout 'admin'
